summary: Stancil argues that while analytics teams obsess over preventing errors, they barely grapple with what to do when mistakes inevitably slip into production. Because analytical errors shape decisions and persist, he questions whether engineering-style blameless retros are sufficient and suggests borrowing from journalism’s accountability. He also proposes rounding metrics to signal uncertainty and reduce the blast radius of inevitable corrections.
money_quotes: For as much as we talk about how to avoid getting things wrong—which is an implicit acknowledgement that we get things wrong a lot—we spend hardly any time talking about what happens when we do.
Despite its sheen of scientific rigor, data is a confidence game; the only currency we have is trust.
Analytical bugs, from bad data to miscalculated metrics to unsound analysis, are different. They stick.
If data teams want the same kind of influence, to be in the room where decisions get made, and to “be heard and have an impact,” we probably have to bear the burden of our mistakes.
No matter how vetted our queries and precise our math, most things that we produce as data teams are estimates.
key_insight: Because analytical errors are inevitable and have lasting consequences, data teams should treat metrics as uncertain estimates and rethink correction practices, potentially emphasizing accountability and rounded communication over engineering-style blamelessness.
